page.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea (OSA) — RepurpOS | OpenSourceMedicine
page.description: Structured alterations and therapeutics for Obstructive Sleep Apnea: molecular targets, clinical biomarkers, phenotypes, and ranked drugs from Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, and DGIdb.
page.hero: Disease intelligence for Obstructive Sleep Apnea: 108 alterations and 237 ranked therapeutics from curated public databases.

gmi_articles.title: Nocturnal oxygen therapy in obstructive sleep apnoea.
Effect of yoga-based lifestyle and dietary modification in overweight individuals with sleep apnea.
Effects of high-intensity interval training on subjective sleep quality and daytime sleepiness in patients with obstructive sleep apnea.
Exercise training has a positive effect on obstructive sleep apnea syndrome.
Low levels of magnesium were associated with a higher CRP concentration in patients with obstructive sleep apnea.
Melatonin mediates intestinal barrier dysfunction and systemic inflammation in moderate-severe OSA patients.
The effect of T'ai Chi and Qigong training on patients with obstructive sleep apnea.
This study provides evidence that serum 25-hydrpoxyvitamin D and obstructive sleep apnea syndrome are related.
7,8-Dihydroxyflavone protects retinal ganglion cells against chronic intermittent hypoxia-induced oxidative stress damage.
Astragaloside IV attenuates chronic intermittent hypoxia-induced myocardial injury by modulating Ca2+ homeostasis.
Citrulline might represent a new pharmacological strategy in OSA patients with high cardiovascular risk.
Honokiol protects pancreatic β cell against high glucose and intermittent hypoxia-induced injury.
Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG strain mitigated the development of obstructive sleep apnea-induced hypertension in a high salt diet.
Paeoniflorin could be a novel strategy for hypoxia-induced brain injury.
Pinocembrin can act as a potential therapeutic strategy for intermittent hypoxia-induced neuroinflammation.
Sesamol alleviates chronic intermittent hypoxia-induced cognitive deficits via inhibiting oxidative stress and inflammation in rats.
Tanshinone IIA inhibited intermittent hypoxia induced neuronal injury through promoting autophagy.

disclaimer: Alterations and therapeutics are aggregated from public databases (Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, DGIdb, and others). Natural agents are candidates from OSMF narrative reviews on opensourcemed.info/chronic-disease.html — not approved treatments. This page is for research orientation only — not medical advice.

remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous resolution without intervention rare; weight loss of 10-15% reduces AHI by 25-50% in obese patients; mild OSA may resolve with weight loss; moderate-severe OSA essentially irreversible without intervention
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: CPAP: eliminates apnoeic events (AHI <5) in 95%+ when used; improves BP 2-3 mmHg, Epworth sleepiness, QoL; mandibular advancement device (MAD): reduces AHI 50-60% in mild-moderate, less effective in severe; bariatric surgery: AHI reduction 80-85% (OSA resolution in 40-50%); hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire): AHI reduction 68% at 12 months
remission.gap_size: Very large — CPAP adherence is the dominant problem; 30-50% of CPAP-prescribed patients are non-adherent; alternative approaches (MAD, positional therapy, weight loss) underused in non-adherent patients
remission.barrier_detail: CPAP adherence is the single largest challenge in sleep medicine; 50% of patients discontinue CPAP within 1-3 years; inadequate follow-up support and lack of alternatives to CPAP for non-adherent patients; hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire) is highly effective in CPAP-intolerant patients but vastly underutilised
remission.last_soc_change: 2024 — tirzepatide showing 60-70% AHI reduction in obese OSA patients (SURMOUNT-OSA trial); 2023 Inspire (HNS) expanded indications; 2014 Inspire FDA approved; CPAP as standard since 1981; essentially unchanged paradigm for 40 years in most clinical settings
remission.outdated_treatments: Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) surgery without HNS assessment (response rates 40-50%, durable in only 30%; should be last resort after HNS evaluation); supplemental oxygen instead of CPAP (treats hypoxia but not arousals/sleep fragmentation); antidepressants for OSA (not indicated as primary treatment)
remission.adjunctive_emerging: GLP-1/GIP agonists (SURMOUNT-OSA: tirzepatide reduced AHI by 60-68% in obese OSA — 2024 landmark result); myofunctional therapy (oropharyngeal exercises — 39% AHI reduction in meta-analysis); positional therapy devices for positional OSA; mouth taping (small series for mouth-breathers)
remission.frontier_signal: Tirzepatide for OSA (FDA approval for OSA without surgery expected 2024-2025 based on SURMOUNT-OSA); AD109 (arousal threshold-raising pharmacotherapy combining atomoxetine + oxybutynin — Phase 3 MARIPOSA trial); reboxetine + antimuscarinic combinations; transoral robotic tongue base reduction
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.

burden.note: Tier from NIH FY funding ÷ US DALYs (or mortality proxy) vs corpus quartiles
